title: 4 3 Areas and Volumes
body:
4.3 AREAS AND VOLUMES


The volume enclosed below by the region of integration Ω in the x-y plane and above by
                the surface z = f (x, y) is given by the double integral
                                          Z Z
                             Volume =           f (x, y)dA .
                                                 Ω

           The area of the region Ω is calculated by setting f (x, y) = 1, that is
                                           Z Z
                                  Area =         1 dA .
                                                 Ω

 The volume between two surfaces z = f1 (x, y) and z = f2 (x, y) with f1 ≥ f2 is given by
                                 Z Z
                      Volume =        (f1 (x, y) − f2 (x, y)) dA .
                                         Ω




Although designed to evaluate volumes the double integral may beR tricked
                                                                     R       into the evalu-
ation of areas by simply replacing f (x, y) with 1. This works since   Ω
                                                                         1 dA  is the volume
above Ω below the horizontal plane z = 1, which is in turn equal to the (area of Ω) × 1

Example 1 Use double integration to find the area bounded by x = y 2 and x = 2y − y 2 .




                                             1
                                                                                        1
                                                                                  F    3
                                                                                         square unit   F


Observe that use of dydx would be a disaster here!
